data.title: FLKR: South Korean Market Nosedives, Wait For The Right Entry Point
data.description:
The South Korean Kospi (KOSPI), one of the world’s hottest stock market indexes with a massive concentration in the top two companies, nosedived after reports of a prolonged war in the Middle East. The authorities are preparing to counter contingency risk as the worst selloff in decades could spillover to the broader market. Furthermore, the crash could spark a significant volatility in the global chips market. South Korea is ranked among the major global semiconductor powerhouses, with a dominating position in the memory chip market.

From an investment prospective, it is crucial to gauge whether the crash indicates a long-lasting downtrend or presenting a price correction to ease valuation. History shows that wars create attractive buying opportunities for investors. However, it is important to choose the right entry point. At present, the selloff appears to be in its early staged due to the expected war timeline of four to five weeks. Moreover, a valuation factor seems overheated because of a stunning AI-driven surge in the last twelve months. Therefore, I initiate coverage of Franklin FTSE South Korea ETF (FLKR) with a Hold rating.

A Review of South Korea Stock Market Crash

The South Korean stock market selloff is deepening as the Kospi plunged into a bear market after losing nearly 20% of its value in the last two days alone, marking the worst selloff since 2008. The intensity of selloff is massive as authorities are preparing to counter the spillover of equity market volatility into other markets. Lee Eog-weon, the South Korean Financial Services Commission Chairman, suggested concerned authorities to prepare contingency plans. During the Legoland credit crisis in 2022, South Korea initially formed a market stabilization fund of more than 50 trillion won to support financial markets.

The current stock market crash begin amid concerns over an impact of a prolonged war on energy supplies and prices. South Korea is among the largest importers of oil and gas from Gulf countries. The country is ranked among the top 10 oil consumers owing to its massive industrial infrastructure. The disruption in oil supplies and increase in prices can negatively impact the country’s inflation, interest rates, and economic growth.

Impact on Semiconductor Industry

South Korea is one of the largest exporters of semiconductor and memory chips. In January, its semiconductor exports rose 102% year over year to $20.54 billion, supported by a hyper demand for artificial intelligence servers. In February, its semiconductor exports soared by 160% to mark a new record high of $25.16 billion. Last year, the U.S. imported $13.8 billion of chips from South Korea, up 30% from the year-ago period. South Korean companies, such as Samsung and SK Hynix, are major players in the global memory chip market.

The U.S. chip stocks begin to feel the pressure on the South Korean chip market. Nvidia (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Broadcom (AVGO), Qualcomm (QCOM) and other plunged sharply. iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), which tracks the performance of NYSE Semiconductor Index, plunged 4% after an initial South Korean stock market crash. The higher production and transportation costs could negatively impact chip prices in months and quarters ahead.

Is it the Time to Invest in South Korean Securities?

Franklin FTSE South Korea ETF is one of the most popular investment vehicles to gain access to the South Korean large and mid-cap stocks. The fund tracks the performance of FTSE South Korea RIC Capped Index. In the last twelve months, FLKR’s share price rallied nearly 130%. In the latest crash, FLKR’s price plunged more than 12% from its late February peak.

Historical trends suggest that wars always create attractive buying opportunities. I believe the current selloff present a buying opportunity. However, the market trends and valuation indicates that the downtrend is in its early stages.

According to President Trump, the war could last four to five weeks. This war is crucial for global markets because it could significantly impact energy prices and global trade. Iran has closed the Strait of Hormuz, which makes up nearly a quarter of the total global oil supplies. The route is also critical for global liquefied natural gas exports. The majority of Gulf countries and Saudi Arabia have suspended their oil and gas supplies due to war.

This situation is adverse for South Korea because the country is sourcing almost 70% of its crude oil and 30% of natural gas from the Middle East.

Kang Sung-jin, economics professor at Korea University, anticipates that the impact of Middle East war on Korea could have significant economic and production consequences. He believes the war is expected disrupt and increase costs for both exports and imports. Similar to 1970 shocks, he believes a prolonged oil supply disruption could significantly boost the entire manufacturing goods cost structure.

On the other hand, FLKR also appears expensive based on its P/E ratio of 19, which is up from 14x at the beginning of year and around 10x its long term average. The fund’s price to book ratio also soared to 2.20 from 1.44 at the beginning of the year. FLKR’s high valuation factor is attributed to the stunning rally in the last twelve months. Overall, it is prudent to wait for the market to bottom out before initiating a new position.

FLKR Portfolio Review and Characteristics

Once the market hits a bottom, Franklin FTSE South Korea ETF could be an attractive investment vehicle, given its portfolio structure and strong characteristics. FLKR’s portfolio comprises large and mid caps. Unlike other ETFs, FLKR limits individual stock holding to a around 20% of its entire portfolio weight to improve the diversification factor and ensure the broader coverage of large and mid caps.

FLKR’s portfolio is spread across all eleven sectors, with a greater concentration in technology sector. The tech stocks account for almost half of its portfolio weight. The industrials, financials and consumer cyclical stocks also represent a healthy weight in its portfolio. Whereas, the health care, consumer defensive, communication, basic materials, energy and utilities sectors collectively represent nearly 14% of its entire portfolio weight.

From the technology sector, Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) and SK Hynix (HXSC.F) collectively make up 43% of its portfolio weight. FLKR also held more than 2.5% of its weight in Samsung Electronics preferred shares. Therefore, the fund’s performance is dominated by these two tech stocks.

In the last twelve months, these two stocks played a key role in FLKR’s blistering price surge. Samsung’s share price rallied more than 210% while SK Hynix shares soared 356% in the last twelve months. Both companies are dominant players in the global memory chip market. In the latest selloff, both stocks are down more than 20% from their recent peaks. Besides these two stocks, the rest of its top 10 stocks, including Hyundai Motor Co, SK Square Co, KB Financial Group and Kia Corporation, also plunged sharply in the current selloff. FLKR’s portfolio is composed of 159 stocks.

FLKR offers a dividend yield of 2.59%. It has lifted its dividends in the last three consecutive years. Its average dividend growth in the past three years was 37%. The fund offers a low expense ratio of 0.09% compared to the median of all ETFs of 0.50%. Its liquidity is also high with 281K in average shares trading volume compared to the median of all ETFs of 42K.

Final Thoughts

It is true that panic selling due to a war and valuation burst creates attractive buying opportunities for investors. However, choosing the right entry time is crucial to achieve hefty returns. At present, the South Korean stock market selloff is in it early stage with a significant downside intensity. Moreover, it is still unclear that how long the Middle East war will last. The uncertainty is also high about the real impact of war on global and Korean markets. Therefore, waiting for a better entry point seems to be a prudent strategy.

data.title: Winners & Losers Of REIT Earnings Season
data.description:
Winners & Losers of REIT Earnings Season

In our Earnings Season Recap, we analyze and highlight the incremental positive and negative takeaways for each property sector, noting the best and worst performers during this earnings season.

Over 200 U.S. REITs and homebuilders have reported fourth-quarter earnings results over the past six weeks, providing critical information on the state of the commercial and residential real estate industry. Overall, REIT earnings results were quite a bit better than consensus expectations - especially in the back-half of earnings season - and avoided the type of high-profile flops that overshadowed recent earnings seasons, with some impressive individual results across a balanced mix of sectors and market cap sizes. Of the 104 equity REITs that provided full-year guidance for Funds from Operations ("FFO"), 64 (62%) reported full-year 2025 FFO that topped the midpoint of their most recent guidance, 24 (23%) matched their guidance, while 16 (15%) missed their guidance - well above the historical average "beat rate" for the fourth quarter of 50-55%. Property-level metrics have posted more favorable trends, with roughly 70% of REITs beating their full-year Net Operating Income ("NOI") outlook - upside driven primarily by lower expenses - consistent with the "disinflation" theme observed in the back-half of 2025.

Before the Iran conflict sent energy prices to multi-year highs and lifted Treasury yields off of multi-year lows, REITs were enjoying a very favorable "goldilocks" macro data trends - cooling inflation alongside "good enough" labor market data - which helped the sector deliver notable earnings season outperformance over the S&P 500. Since the start of earnings season in mid-January, the Equity REIT Index (VNQ) is higher by 5.7%, outperforming the -0.7% decline from the S&P 500 during this time. Despite this recent outperformance, REITs still maintain a historically-wide 60-percentage-point underperformance gap versus the S&P since early 2022. Solid trends across most of the real estate sector come alongside a slightly weaker-than-expected set of earnings results across the broader U.S. equity market, particularly among the mega-cap tech giants that had shouldered the market gains in recent years. FactSet reports that 73% of S&P 500 constituents have topped consensus Earnings Per Share ("EPS") estimates - slightly below the 5-year average of 78% and the 10-year average of 76%.

Notable upside standouts this earnings season include Data Center, Senior Housing, Farmland, and Retail REITs. Senior Housing REITs - the perennial performance leader in recent years- reported another stellar quarter, with an equally impressive 2026 outlook for 15% NOI growth in their critical Senior Housing Operating Portfolio ("SHOP") segment. Data Center REITs continued to prove they could hang with the tech “big boys” amid the AI boom, maintaining near-zero vacancy and impressive pricing power. Results from Retail REIT across the strip center, regional mall, and single-tenant formats were a pleasant upside surprise, as limited new supply has kept occupancy rates near record-highs, and rent spreads in double-digits despite a slight demand moderation. Net Lease REITs, meanwhile, have accelerated their pace of acquisition activity as cap rates have ticked higher, resulting in increasingly attractive investment spreads. Speciality and agriculture-focused REIT sectors shined in the back-half of earnings season: Farmland and Cannabis REITs showed signs of operator stabilization after a post-pandemic slump in crop prices. Billboard REITs showed no signs of cooling in demand for Out-of-Home advertising, while Cold Storage REITs halted their cold streak of quarterly "misses" with a decent quarter. Hotel REITs also posted surprisingly solid results despite a soft stretch of travel demand in late 2025. Manufactured Housing REITs were the long residential sector to earn a spot among the "winners", with impressive performance in their core MH segment.

This season’s leaderboard is topped by REITs that agreed to deals to be taken private, reinforcing the “REIT Exodus” trend that has persisted over the past several quarters. Small-cap net lease REIT Peakstone (PKST) has surged near 50% after it agreed to be acquired by Brookfield Asset Management in an all-cash deal at $21/share - a 34% premium to its last closing price. PKST owns 70 industrial properties across 18 states, with a focus on the Industrial Outdoor Storage ("IOS") segment - a niche category consisting of outdoor yards used to store large equipment and materials. Homebuilder Tri Pointe (TPH) has jumped nearly 40% after it agreed to be acquired by Japan's Sumitomo Forestry for in an all-cash $4.5B deal at $47/share - a 29% premium to its prior closing price. Veris Residential (VRE) – a small-cap NYC-metro focused apartment REIT that owns roughly 6,500 units across 17 properties – has rallied nearly 30% after reaching a deal to be taken private by an investor consortium led by Affinius Capital in an all-cash transaction valued at $19.00 per share. Since 2022, a total of 42 REITs have been acquired, liquidated, or formally put up for sale, as deep and persistent public-market NAV discounts relative to private valuations continue to incentivize boards and management teams to pursue strategic alternatives aimed at narrowing this valuation gap and maximizing shareholder value.

While there weren't any major bombshells, we have seen a handful of disappointing results and softer-than-expected trends across a handful of sectors. Results from Office REITs showed that while property-level trends have inflected positively—evidenced by improved leasing volumes, modest occupancy gains, and stabilizing in-place rents—bottom-line profitability remains constrained by elevated leverage, higher interest expense, and balance sheets still healing from years of value erosion. Results from Residential REITs - both apartment and single-family rental - were also modestly disappointing, with results showing the early-year reacceleration in rent growth completely reversed in the back half of the year, even in the tight Northeast and Midwest markets. Results from Commercial Mortgage REIT were a setback after several encouraging quarters, as pockets of distress lingered in office, multifamily, and lab space loans, stretching dividend coverage and prompting a pair of cuts. For Residential Mortgage REITs, however, the underlying earnings were quite encouraging, highlighted by an unexpected 20% dividend increase from a name that's already paying a double-digit dividend yield. Results from Cell Tower REITs were relatively soft, as expected, given the DISH default and ongoing carrier consolidation. Industrial REITs showed that fundamentals remain steady outside of the still-struggling West Coast, but the upward inflection in fundamentals isn't expected until late 2026 amid lingering oversupply. Results from Medical Office REITs were a step in the right direction, with upside surprises from the core MOB segment offset by lab space weakness.

Winners of REIT Earnings Season

Winner #1: Senior Housing: (Final Grade: A)

Positives: Stellar senior housing outlook for 2026 for 15%+ NOI growth after 15-20% growth in 2025; External growth activity broadening beyond WELL; Skilled nursing tenant rent coverage improves to decade-highs.

Negatives: Low-single-digit NOI growth in non-SHOP segments; Slight FFO miss from CTRE on equity raise (but still impressive double-digit growth); Mixed results from NHI & LTC amid continued push into SHOP segment.

Big Winners: American Healthcare (AHR), Welltower (WELL)

Big Losers: None

Winner #2: Data Center: (Final Grade: A)

Positives: Impressive 2026 FFO outlook for double-digit growth; resilient pricing power despite booming investment in capacity; EQIX 10% dividend hike; DLR has its "mojo" back after a half-decade of zero growth.

Negatives: Choppy hypersale leasing volume (primarily on capacity constraints), FX headwinds on recent dollar strength; Fermi has tumbled since its IPO after the anchor tenant backed out of Project Matador.

Big Winners: Equinix (EQIX), Iron Mountain (IRM)

Big Losers: Fermi (FRMI)

Winner #3: Net Lease: (Final Grade: A)

Positives: Sector-leading 10 FFO beats with no misses; Rebound in acquisitions as investment spreads become more accretive, No major new credit issues; Strength from value-oriented retail, automotive, experiential.

Negatives: Typical mix of tenant-specific issues in softer segments (pharmacy, restaurants, theater); Softer growth rates in 2025 & 2026 from the sector's "big boys" (O, NNN) as size makes it harder to move the needle.

Big Winners: Peakstone (PKST), Curbline (CURB), Postal Realty (PSTL)

Big Losers: Generation Income (GIPR)

Winner #4: Billboard: (Final Grade: A-)

Positives: Rebound in ad spending among national advertisers, Big outperformance in transit segment (led by New York MTA), Continued benefit from digital billboard conversions, World Cup & political tailwinds in 2026.

Negatives: Tough comps from political spending last year; Other weaker segments: telecom, wine/beer, government, and automotive; Core billboard segment (non-transit) was softer in 2025.

Big Winners: Outfront (OUT), Lamar (LAMR)

Big Losers: None

Winner #5: Regional Mall: (Final Grade: A-)

Positives: Upper-end consumer remains strong and still spending; Tanger's record-high leasing volumes & occupancy rate; Simon's 5% domestic NOI growth with same 2026 outlook; MAC beginning to get its act together.

Negatives: Announced retail closures in recent months have been more mall-heavy relative to recent trends; CBL's 2026 FFO forecast suggests it's not out of the woods yet; Lower-end consumer cited as segment of softness.

Big Winners: Tanger (SKT), Simon (SPG)

Big Losers: None

Winner #6: Farmland: (Final Grade: A-)

Positives: Strong harvest & fewer drought conditions help farmers after several tough seasons; Impressive rebound from FPI since 2023; FPI's 10% dividend hike; LAND stabilizing via re-leasing and direct farming operations.

Negatives: Grain prices still near four-year lows & hovering around pre-pandemic levels; Lingering farmland vacancies; Tariff & trade uncertainty.

Big Winners: Gladstone Land (LAND), Farmland Partners (FPI)

Big Losers: None

Winner #7: Strip Centers: (Final Grade: A-)

Positives: Double-digit leasing spreads for 15th-straight quarter; Occupancy rates still near record-highs; small-shop occupancy improving; Handful of dividend hikes, including UE (+11%) and IVT (+5%).

Negatives: Still relatively quiet on the acquisitions front (leaning heavily into redevelopment); Noisy report from AKR on one-time factors; some short-term occupancy headwinds from recent bankruptcies (Rite Aid).

Big Winners: Brixmor (BRX), Kite Realty (KRG),

Big Losers: Site Centers (SITC)

Winner #8: Casino: (Final Grade: B+)

Positives: GLPI forecasting 2026 will be the strongest year of growth since 2019; Steady FFO beats driven by mid-sized M&A deals and development financing (VICI buys Golden Entertainment, GLPI buys Bally's Lincoln).

Negatives: Lingering concerns over Las Vegas weakness (visitor traffic down 5-8% y/y); Key operators struggling (CZR, PENN); Vegas weakness due to steep dip in inbound international tourism (Canada especially).

Big Winner: Gaming & Leisure Properties (GLPI)

Big Loser: None

Winner #9: Hotel (Final Grade: B+)

Positives: Upside surprises: 9 of 10 hotel REITs reported 2025 FFO growth above prior guidance (+1.5% average vs. -3.2% expected); Group demand remained the standout, particularly in upscale urban / resort portfolios.

Negatives: Tone for 2026 turned more cautious: five REITs guide to negative FFO growth; Lingering margin and cost pressures; Economy segment remains soft; International travel remains weak & Middle East conflict won't help.

Big Winners: Chatham Lodging (CLDT), Diamondrock (DRH)

Big Losers: Braemar Hotels (BHR), Ashford Hospitality (AHT)

Winner #10: Manufactured Housing: (Final Grade: B+)

Positives: Core manufactured housing portfolio continues to outperform; Solid mid-single digit rent increases expected in 2026; Sun getting its "mojo" back as U.K. portfolio stabilizes; Dividend hikes from SUI (8%) and ELS (5%).

Negatives: Miss from UMH on equity issuance; RV segment remains weak - especially from Canadians; Still quiet on the domestic M&A front; Softness in lower-end consumer will weigh on ability to push rents.

Big Winner: Sun Communities (SUI)

Big Loser: UMH Properties (UMH)

REIT Earnings Season Losers

Loser#1: Office: (Final Grade: C)

Negatives: Balance sheet repairs pushing the upward FFO inflection in 2027 despite improved property-level trends; No bottoming yet in California market; Mamdani's property tax proposal hit NYC-focused names (SLG, BXP ESRT).

Positives: Best quarter of leasing volume since mid-2010s; Occupancy rates are finally bottoming; Continued Sunbelt outperformance (CUZ, HIW); Solid government/defense leasing activity as DOGE concerns fade.

Big Winners: COPT Defense (CDP), Easterly Government (DEA)

Big Losers: Kilroy (KRC), SL Green (SLG), Hudson Pacific (HPP)

Loser #2: Commercial Mortgage REITs (Final Grade: C)

Negatives: Worst BVPS decline since 2021; Loan downgrades in multifamily, lab space, and office; Stretched dividend coverage prompts FBRT dividend cut & KREF to "review policy;" No bottom yet for struggling CMTG or GPMT.

Positives: ABR shows signs of stabilization after rough 2025, ARI's acquisition by Athene signals some M&A appetite & confirms deep NAV discounts vs. private markets, Solid quarter from the sector's "big boys" (STWD & BXMT).

Big Winners: Arbor Realty (ABR), Apollo Commercial (ARI)

Big Losers: Granite Point (GPMT), Claros Mortgage (CMTG)

Loser #3: Apartment: (Final Grade: C+)

Negatives: New lease rent trends (-5.2%) worsen after rebound in early 2026, Softness spreads from Sunbelt to Mid-Atlantic and West Coast, Early signs of immigration impact on demand (especially at lower-tier).

Positives: Renewal rent growth (+3.5%) still holding up, Favorable expense control trends (moderate property tax and insurance increases), Veris Residential gets bought out (and others - CSR, ELME - still in play).

Big Winners: Veris Residential (VRE)

Big Losers: Elme Communities (ELME), Clipper Realty (CLPR)

Loser #4: Cell Tower: (Final Grade: C+)

Negatives: Material hit from DISH/Echostar default with pending legal fight; 3rd-straight year of low-to-negative FFO growth expected in 2026; No near-term catalysts to drive upward inflection amid ongoing carrier consolidation.

Positives: SBAC's 13% dividend hike; Guidance beats on cost savings with more efficiencies planned; AMT's data center outperformance; Chatter on 6G networks helps lift sentiment; Valuations hovering around historic lows.

Big Winners: SBA Communications (SBAC), American Tower (AMT)

Big Losers: None

Loser #5: Single Family Rental: (Final Grade: B-)

Negatives: Policy risk remains key overhang as White House advances the institutional homebuying ban; Deceleration in new lease rates across all regions; Sunbelt rental trends remain especially weak.

Positives: Favorable expense controls (modest insurance and property tax increases); Continued push into Built-to-Rent internal development to offset acquisitions risk; Renewals remain buoyant; AMH dividend hike (10%).

Big Winners: None

Big Losers: American Homes (AMH)

Loser #6: Self-Storage REITs: (Final Grade: B)

Negatives: Occupancy rates remain under pressure; Home moving rates (which drives storage demand) still historically low; Still dealing with pockets of oversupply (especially in Sunbelt) after COVID-era development boom.

Positives: New lease rates ("Street Rates") finally stabilized after 12 quarters of declines; Favorable expense controls (modest advertising, labor, and property taxes); Signs of recovery from NSA after rough 2022-2024.

Big Winners: Public Storage (PSA), CubeSmart (CUBE)

Big Losers: SmartStop (SMA), Global Self Storage (SELF)

Loser #7: Medical Office / Lab Space:(Final Grade: B)

Negatives: Lab space fundamentals remain very soft with positive inflection not expected until 2027 at the earliest; Disappointing 2026 FFO outlook given the healthy MOB property-level trends; Misses from XRN and SILA.

Positives: Solid Medical Office Building ("MOB") leasing volume & rent spreads; MOB guidance sees 2-3% Same-Store NOI growth; Signs of stabilization at MPT after brutal stretch from 2022-2025.

Big Winners: Universal Health (UHT), Medical Properties (MPT)

Big Losers: Alexandria (ARE), Chiron / Global Medical (XRN)

Loser #8: Industrial: (Final Grade: B)

Negatives: Market rent growth still negative (-4% Y/Y) with inflection not expected until 2H26, Setback in sluggish SoCal market; Smaller tenants still delaying major lease decisions, PLYM's sale at a discount to prior offer.

Positives: Reacceleration in leasing volume and rent spreads in "middle-America" markets following tariff-related pause; Tenant recognition of the need for supply chain diversification.

Big Winners: Prologis (PLD), First Industrial (FR)

Big Losers: LXP Industrial (LXP), Rexford (REXR)

Loser #9: Residential Mortgage REITs (Final Grade: B)

Positives: Much-improved dividend coverage after two-straight quarters of increased Book Values (+2.0%) & EPS (+3.8%) increases; Agency mREITs report +5% average BVPS increase and +2-4% QTD; CIM's dividend hike.

Negatives: Relative weakness from long-outperforming MSR segment, highly competitive refinancing environment for services-heavy mREITs (PMT, RITM); TWO back at pre-acquisition value on UWMC share price slump.

Big Winners: Chimera (CIM), Redwood (RWT), MFA Financial (MFA)

Big Losers: Two Harbors (TWO), Rithm Capital (RITM)

Loser #10: Cold Storage (Final Grade: B)

Negatives: Both REITs see double-digit FFO dip in 2026 (as expected); Not out of the woods yet in pressure on both pricing and economic occupancy as customers continue to manage inventory tightly.

Positives: Stabilization in occupancy rates; Strong harvest season helping; Finally a "beat" after several downbeat quarters; New leasing volume has been a bright spot, offset by reductions in the base business and churn.

Big Winner: Lineage (LINE)

Big Loser: None

Earnings Recap: Strong Results, But Will It Matter?

Overall, REIT earnings results were quite a bit better than consensus expectations - especially in the back-half of earnings season - and avoided the type of high-profile flops that overshadowed recent earnings seasons, with some impressive individual results across a balanced mix of sectors and market cap sizes. Upside standouts this earnings season include Senior Housing, Data Center, and Retail REITs. Speciality and agriculture-focused REIT sectors shined in the back-half of earnings season. There were no major bombshells this earnings season, but Apartment, Office, and Commercial Mortgage REITs have been the notable laggards. Multifamily rents softened in late 2025, particularly in the Sunbelt. Office REIT results showed progress at the property-level, but lingering headwinds on bottom-line FFO from balance sheet repair. Two REITs accepted takeover offers - surging more than 30% as a result - indicating that the public market discount is as wide as ever, and the "REIT Exodus" trend will persist until public REITs can close this valuation gap. And while these REITs should be commended for pursuing the path that maximizes shareholder value, investors are seeing the investible universe shrink and more assets pulled out of the public markets - an unfortunate byproduct of REITs being "too cheap" for too long.

For an in-depth analysis of all real estate sectors, check out all of our quarterly reports: Apartments, Homebuilders, Manufactured Housing, Student Housing, Single-Family Rentals, Cell Towers, Casinos, Industrial, Data Centers, Malls, Healthcare, Net Lease, Shopping Centers, Hotels, Billboards, Office, Farmland, Storage, Timber, Mortgage, and Cannabis.

Disclosure: Hoya Capital Real Estate advises two Exchange-Traded Funds listed on the NYSE. In addition to any long positions listed below, Hoya Capital is long all components in the Hoya Capital Housing 100 Index and in the Hoya Capital High Dividend Yield Index. Index definitions and a complete list of holdings are available on our website.

data.title: Black Rock Coffee Bar: On The Path To 1,000 Stores, Rising Margin Profile
data.description:
2026 has been an incredibly challenging stock market to invest in. Investors are being bombarded by a flurry of negative news, ranging from the possibility of a "SaaSpocalypse" that has pulled down software stocks to rising geopolitical tensions and macroeconomic uncertainty that has hammered just about everything else. In my view, the best move here is to keep our heads down while taking advantage of oversold conditions to pick up shares of high-quality businesses at a discount, especially in the small- and mid-cap space.

Black Rock Coffee Bar, Inc. (BRCB), the Arizona-headquartered coffee chain that is gradually expanding its footprint across the western U.S., is a great example of a stock whose recent share price performance is not at all aligned to its fundamental reality. Despite strong same-store sales growth that has defied industry incumbents, shares are down more than 30% since the start of the year, which, in my view, creates a tremendous buying opportunity in an oversold name. Black Rock just rose nearly 10% after reporting strong Q4 results and a healthy FY26 outlook, and in my view, this is just the beginning of a longer rebound.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Black Rock in December, when the stock was trading at $22 per share. While I acknowledge that my buy call was ill-timed, I'm not wavering from my position. At the time, Black Rock was a high-growth company trading at a fair (if slightly rich) price, but today, the stock is very much a value play amid very strong results. Amid clear evidence that rivals like Starbucks (SBUX) continue to lose ground, I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating on Black Rock.

Since value is now one of the core reasons to be long here, let's start with a discussion on the company's latest valuation multiples against its most recently printed outlook. At current share prices near $15, Black Rock trades at a market cap of just $412.6 million. Netting off the $28.4 million of cash and $26.8 million of debt on the company's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $411.0 million.

For FY26, Black Rock has guided to $255-$257 million in revenue, representing 28% y/y total revenue growth. That forecast is predicated on mid-single-digit same-store sales growth, alongside 36 net-new store openings (which is 20% unit growth versus 181 stores at the end of Q4, and slightly more than the 32 locations that the company opened in FY25). The company is also expecting all-in adjusted EBITDA of $33.5-$34.5 million, the midpoint of which is 21% y/y growth at a 13% margin.

Black Rock trades at a 12.1x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA multiple. For a company that is currently achieving high single-digit comps growth (where peers like Starbucks are growing closer to the low single digits), is expanding margins, and is also growing adjusted EBITDA at a >20% y/y clip, I'd say this is quite a deal for the company.

We note as well that as of today, Black Rock still only has 3-4 main markets where it is densely operational: Washington, Oregon, Arizona, and Texas. The company believes it has white space to expand to over 1,000 locations by 2035 by filling in expansion/growth markets and new states in which the company doesn't have a single location yet:

To me, with Black Rock clearly showcasing strong sales in its existing markets, there is plenty of opportunity for this company to take advantage of its wide-open opportunity.

Stay long here and use the post-earnings dip as a buying opportunity.

Q4 Recap

Let's now go through Black Rock's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Black Rock's revenue grew 25% y/y to $53.6 million, which accelerated slightly versus just 24% y/y growth in Q3. The company's growth, meanwhile, was fueled by both new store expansion plus same-store sales growth. The company opened 12 new stores in the quarter, bringing total period-end locations to 181, up 22% y/y.

Where I am most optimistic, however, is the fact that Black Rock's same-store sales continue to sail well above peers. As a reminder, same-store sales measures the revenue growth from existing locations, excluding the contribution from newly opened units.

As shown in the chart above, the company notched a 9.3% same-store sales growth rate in Q4, which softened only slightly from >10% in the past two quarters (but was materially higher than FY24). Part of the compression in comps rates was a tougher comparison against 2H'24 results last year; on a 2-year stack basis, Q4 comps grew 19% y/y, in line with Q3 and accelerating from the first half of FY25.

The most relevant comparison is against Black Rock's core competitors. Starbucks (SBUX), the world's largest coffee chain, showcased only 4% same-store sales growth (comp sales) in its fiscal Q1/December quarter. Meanwhile, Dutch Bros (BROS) (which is often considered a closer direct competitor to Black Rock Coffee Bar thanks to its more local origins and drive-through focus) also showcased only 7.7% comps growth in Q4, two points below Black Rock.

It's undeniable from these comparisons that Black Rock continues to achieve rising market share in a very crowded and fragmented coffee industry.

The company is also doing so while building up its profit margins. Despite tariffs and coffee price inflation, the company's cost of beverage and packaging fell 180 bps y/y to 27.4% of revenue. The company also shaved 70 bps of labor expenses as a percentage of revenue.

As a result, store-level profit margins (an industry measure that excludes corporate overhead) expanded 230 bps y/y to 29.4%. Adjusted EBITDA, meanwhile, grew 52% y/y to $6.5 million, which hit a 12.1% margin that improved 210 bps y/y.

Key Takeaways

Though Black Rock Coffee Bar remains very much an "under the radar" stock, the company is clearly doing well despite a tougher macroeconomic climate. Same-store sales growth continues to index well above peers, while the company is shaving costs across line items to produce substantial adjusted EBITDA growth. Take advantage of the stock's recent compression in multiples to "Buy."

data.title: CrowdStrike Beats, But AI Fears Still Win
data.description:
CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (CRWD) reported Q4 '26 results that slightly exceeded Wall Street expectations, while its outlook for fiscal 2027 also came in modestly above estimates. The stock is flat in pre-market trading, up 0.4%, building on the 1.7% surge the day prior, as the overall market pulled back on Tuesday. The stock erased these gains right before the market opened on Wednesday, and the stock dipped around 1%, before rebounding and flying 2% at open, trading above $400.

That didn’t last long, and the stock retreated to the $390 level, as it tries to make its way to the next near term resistance level at the EMA21.

A lot of the positives were priced into print, with the stock up over 5.3% over the past five days. Still, CrowdStrike, along with other cybersecurity stocks, has gone out of the market’s favor, and it specifically started after Anthropic (ANTHRO) unveiled a new Claude capability focused on identifying and fixing software vulnerabilities, reviving concerns that agentic AI tools could compress demand and pricing for parts of the traditional security software stack.

That headline sparked a sector-wide selloff, and CrowdStrike got caught in the crossfire.

The fear is worth unpacking, but I think the market is conflating two very different things. An AI tool that scans code for vulnerabilities is not replacing the Falcon platform. It is not replacing endpoint detection, threat intelligence, or incident response. CrowdStrike controls roughly 16.8% of the endpoint security market, second only to Microsoft's (MSFT) 28.6%, according to an IDC report, Worldwide Modern Endpoint Security Market Shares, 2024, and that position wasn't built on a single product, but really on a platform that keeps expanding.

The outage hangover is real. The competitive environment from Palo Alto Networks’ (PANW) bundling, also known as platformization strategy, is real, the core business is not broken, and this earnings call went on to prove exactly that. So, while the company's fundamentals remain strong, investor expectations are even stronger, and at these multiples, even a clean beat didn’t move the stock.

Still, I think there's more fuel in the tank for CrowdStrike, especially as the stock seems to be gaining momentum over the past few trading sessions. While the market is wary of the Iran war that seems to be escalating, and while I do think that CrowdStrike could move in sympathy with the fearful market, I think the risk-reward is favorable at current levels.

Earnings:

The company posted EPS of $1.12 per share, slightly above consensus at $1.10 per share and up 38% year over year, and revenue at $1.31 billion, up 23% year over year and again narrowly beating consensus of $1.297 billion. Management highlighted strong demand across the platform, particularly in its core endpoint security segment, which accelerated for the second consecutive quarter as enterprises increasingly adopt AI-driven cybersecurity solutions. Growth was also supported by strong adoption of the company’s next-generation identity and SIEM offerings, both of which delivered record levels of new annual recurring revenue (ARR).

Total ARR, which is a key metric for subscription-based software businesses, was up 24% to $5.25 billion, slightly above consensus, while net new ARR was up another 47% to $331 million, significantly above consensus. Looking forward to FY27, CrowdStrike guided for adjusted EPS of about $4.84 and revenue between $5.867 billion and $5.927 billion, both modestly ahead of consensus. The company also expects ARR to grow roughly 24% to $6.49 billion, supported in part by recent acquisitions such as SGNL and Seraphic.

The Falcon Flex flywheel is the biggest catalyst going forward. According to the company’s CEO Goerge Kurtz,

The proof of Falcon adoption success is in the re-Flex. Customers are using what they buy and expanding their Flex commitments. More than 380 Flex accounts have already re-Flexed, representing more than 23% of the Flex customer base, up from 5% in Q1. The average ARR lift after a re-Flex is 26%, happening on average within 7 months. And the platform adoption grows even further from there. We're now tracking the number of customers who are repeat re-Flexers. Nearly 100 customers have re-Flexed multiple times. The multiple-time re-Flex cohort now represents approximately 6% of total Flex customers and over 1/4 of all re-Flex customers. Our multiple-time re-Flexers, on average, have an ARR lift of an additional 48% from their initial Flex subscription.

This is becoming a compounding machine. The Q1 pipeline grew 49% year-over-year, which also adds real conviction behind the guide. AI is a structural tailwind, and sensors are already detecting more than 1,800 distinct AI applications running on enterprise endpoints. Every one of those applications is an attack surface that needs protection, and AIDR grew 5x in a single quarter from virtually nothing. As enterprise AI adoption accelerates, CrowdStrike becomes more necessary, not less.

Risks and valuation:

Valuation remains the ceiling, and the AI disruption fear isn't going away. Dan Ives literally asked about it on the call, and it's been the single biggest overhang on the stock since the Claude vulnerability scanning narrative hit, as previously mentioned. According to Kurtz,

In cybersecurity, you simply cannot have a hallucination. You can't prompt twice. It's first time final. It's the difference between thwarting an adversary or experiencing a breach. Cybersecurity is a unique paradigm. Success for us and our customer is did we stop a breach.

Still, the market will keep asking this question every quarter until there's definitive proof that frontier models aren't eating into the TAM.

Another risk here the the execution risk on M&A. SGNL, Seraphic, Onum, Pangea, totaling four acquisitions in quick succession. Kurtz has a strong track record of integrating acquisitions natively into Falcon, but that's a lot of plates spinning at once, and the $74 million to $80 million in integration OpEx is hitting the P&L in FY27 before the revenue contribution fully materializes.

On valuation, the stock is expensive, as forward EV/Sales of 16x and forward PEG of 3.69x remain above peers.

The stock, on the other hand, is outperforming on growth metrics, as seen below.

CrowdStrike's stock is currently trading below all its EMAs, but is trying to break the EMA21 at $398. I would like to see the stock reclaim that level and hold, along with RSI recovering to the mid-50 levels. While I do think that the market is wary of the geopolitical tension in the Middle East and the potential for a broader Iran war to trigger a flight from high-beta tech, the structural setup for CrowdStrike, in my opinion, remains one of the most compelling in the enterprise software ecosystem.

Fears are around Claude, or others, simply coding away vulnerabilities, ignoring the reality of the active adversary. But securing an enterprise is about monitoring a living, breathing network, and I don’t think AI will take away that, yet.

data.title: Navios Maritime Partners Remains A Buy After A 100% Rally
data.description:
I’ve covered Navios Maritime Partners (NMM) before. In my initial article I outlined why I considered it a strong investment and a low risk opportunity; since then, the stock has returned more than 100%. Q4 results were great and above analysts’ estimates, and I expect a similar outcome in Q1. This, together with the steady repurchases, still makes it an interesting buy.

Background

Navios is the largest listed diversified shipping company by number of vessels and second largest by DWT with exposure across drybulk, containers, and tankers.

The company has continued to actively manage its fleet, as mentioned in my past articles. Navios has taken advantage of the elevated second hand VLCC prices to divest two of them generating $136.5 million in gross proceeds. This reinforces management’s strategy of selling older tonnage and reinvesting into newbuilds. At the same time, Navios now has 26 newbuild vessels following the delivery of an LR2 tanker and it has continued ordering new vessels with two additional Capesize vessels with delivery scheduled for 2028 and 2029. Following Navios strategy, these vessels have already been chartered out for five years with a floor rate of $25,000 per day plus a 50% profit sharing component, securing downside protection while maintaining upside exposure.

This is a key characteristic of Navios. While the fleet is balanced across these segments, the company’s earnings continue to be mainly driven by drybulk, as containership vessels are on long term fixed contracts and tankers have only limited index linked days. Most of the linked index or spot exposure is in the drybulk segment, which provides most of the volatility in quarterly earnings.

This approach, combined with the company’s contract structure, has resulted in a backlog of approximately $3.8 billion providing strong visibility for the coming years.

The company’s capital allocation strategy continues to prioritize deleveraging first until it reaches a net LTV target between 20% to 25%, followed by fleet renewal, and then shareholder returns. While the leverage target is still far away as newbuilds deliveries increase the leverage, the company continues its steady buybacks of around $10 million per quarter. These repurchases are highly accretive and contribute to long term value creation as Navios trades considerably below its NAV.

This quarter the company also increased its quarterly dividend by 20%, to $0.06 per share. Given the low dividend, I consider this to be mainly testimonial and without impact.

Financial Position and Valuation

Navios has a strong financial position with $411 million in cash and $2.2 billion in debt, resulting in a net LTV around 30%. In 2022 this ratio was at 45% and the fleet was older. Moreover, as tanker and drybulk vessels values are increasing, the LTV will be further reduced which could compensate some of the debt increase when the newbuild delivers.

Q4 earnings exceeded analysts’ estimates as I anticipated in my last article, and I expect a similar outcome with Q1 results. However, Q1 results are more difficult to estimate because the company provided full year figures:

Despite this, I believe Q1 will be similar to Q4. Q4 2025 realized rate was $25,500 per day and contracted revenue is already above that. During Q1 the tanker indexed rates should provide a nice tailwind as rates are being outstanding. On the other hand, drybulk will be a drag as usually Q1 is the softer month, but this year rates have been astonishingly strong and just slightly below Q4 rates. This is why I believe the company will achieve at least an EPS of $3, considerably above analysts’ estimates:

Regarding valuation, NAV per share has increased since Q3 report, moving from $137.8 per share to $140.3 at the end of Q4.

Given the strong cash generation, the last few weeks asset prices increases and the repurchases, I expect Q1 NAV to be above Q4 2024. Despite the stock moving almost 40% YTD, it still trades at just 0.5x times NAV.

Management has demonstrated its commitment to repurchase after more than one year with a steady pace, and it recently increased its testimonial dividend. I expect they will increase its repurchase pace if the stock still trades at a meaningful discount to NAV and leverage approaches its target.

In my last article I valued the company at 0.6x times NAV; however, given the consistent capital allocation, and the company great prospects I am adjusting it to 0.7x times, giving a fair value of $100 per share. This represents about a 40% upside.

Risks

The main risks remain largely unchanged. The rate volatility, particularly in the drybulk segment, continues to be the main factor affecting earnings. While this exposure provides upside in strong markets, it will also negatively impact earnings if rates decline.

The second main risk is the capital allocation. While I expect some newbuilds, if the company starts a large newbuild program I believe it will not be well received by the market. Alternatively, if the company stops buybacks, it will negatively affect management perception and the discount will get bigger, impacting the valuation.

Conclusion

Despite Navios stock rising by more than 80% in the last year, it continues to be an interesting buy.

This overperformance is mainly due to the change in perception regarding management. If management continues its steady course, the stock should continue to close the valuation gap against peers. However, to move closer to or above NAV, the capital allocation should shift to higher dividends in the future.

data.title: APA Corp: Redefined By Operational Efficiencies And Middle East Turmoil
data.description:
Thesis

APA (APA) has reinvented itself in just a short 12 months. Almost exactly a year ago, I wrote a piece on the company to discuss its poor performance, generating just $841m in free cash flow. However, thanks to an aggressive cost improvement program, the company generated over $1B in 2025 free cash flow.

This performance was generated amidst a year-long decline in crude oil prices, giving credence to the company’s efforts. APA has not-so-simply, reinvented itself as a top tier operator.

The company has also assembled more ammunition through its gas development program in both the Permian and Egypt. The company has the optionality to access strong international pricing through improved Waha pricing, LNG export marketing, and even the Egyptian government.

Thanks to these profitability efforts, APA still trades at solid free cash flow yields. This value serves as a nice complement to the long-term potential of the company’s Suriname project. This project is expected to increase the company’s free cash flow by 50% in 2029, making APA an easy BUY recommendation.

Earnings Review

APA produced another strong quarter, generating $425m in free cash flow. The strong sequential performance was aided by a generally light quarter of capital spend.

For the full year, APA was able to generate $1B in FCF, despite declining oil prices over the period. This is a 19% improvement over 2024 levels and is a strong testament to the value creation efforts that APA has conducted over the course of the year.

During 2025, the company made significant strides in cost control. As shown below, APA has reduced its cost profile by $300 million, while holding total production volumes flat y-o-y. This is a very significant milestone as it accounts for roughly 30% of the company's 2025 free cash flow generation.

The bulk of these savings were expected to be derived by reducing the capital intensity of the company's drilling program. Thus far the company has been fairly successful in this endeavor, driving total well costs down by mid-single digits in the Permian.

This results in a net improvement of $300m in annual drilling and completion capital. By making each well more capital efficient, the company is also able to pull in economic inventory to refresh its development program.

APA now estimates that it holds over 1,000 well locations that generate greater than a 10% return at $50 WTI. At current activity levels, this translates into nearly 8 years of highly profitable activity. This figure expands to 1,500 wells and 11 years of profitable activity at $60/barrel, making for a rather robust inventory set.

More importantly, APA made a conscience effort to delineate that innovation is still alive and well in this industry. That same innovation continues to refresh the backlog of economical and technically available projects.

The most prominent development of this earnings season (across multiple operators) is the test of the deeper Barnett shale play. APA will be testing this zone with a four well package as it works toward driving this interval from “technical upside” to its growing economic inventory package.

This type of innovation is giving the Permian staying power despite the “peak shale” narrative. In particular, using this approach APA’s technical team has allowed the company’s inventory replenishment to exceed full year production. The company improved reserves by nearly 9% y-o-y, an impressive improvement by any standard.

Significant Natural Gas Leverage

APA also has an underappreciated natural gas portfolio. This stems from an operational setback years ago at its Alpine High acreage. This acreage is in one of the gassier benches of the Permian and has struggled to remain productive in recent years due to extremely low Waha hub pricing.

However, 2026 looks to be an inflection point for Waha as over 4 BCF/d of long-haul pipeline capacity comes online. While this capacity is back weighted toward the end of 2026, the improvement in the forward index is obvious. As a result, Permian gas should yield significantly more value for producers in 2027.

The value for APA is significant on a portfolio that generates 0.5 BCF/d. In 2025, the company reported a realized price of just $1.02/MCF. Boosting this value to even $2/MCF increases the company's free cash flow by $175m. This creates a straightforward path to double digit free cash flow growth in 2027.

The company has also had massive success on its gas development program in Egypt. In early 2025, the company was able to broker a deal with the Egyptian government that gave APA access to premium natural gas pricing.

The company is expecting a 45% price improvement over 2024 levels, thereby driving the returns on natural gas drilling to parity with oil development. As a result, nearly 50% of the company's Egyptian development will be focused on liquids rich gas development. For the full year, APA expects to realize $4.25/MCF, creating a win-win for both parties.

Due to the higher prices, the company expects to increase natural gas production by approximately 15% in 2026. However, recent developments could push that production even higher. Following the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a significant portion of global LNG has been removed from the market.

This development could help APA in two ways. The most obvious would be the incentive to deliver more gas to the Egyptian market. Secondarily, the LNG spot price is expected to increase significantly if these cargoes are held out of the market for any significant period of time. This will allow the company's natural gas market division to extract even higher premiums on its Waha priced gas.

Prior to the conflict, APA expected to generate $650m in pre-tax cash flow from its marketing division. This is accomplished through a combination of LNG contracts with Cheniere (LNG) and leveraging the differential between the Waha hub and the US gulf coast.

Now that LNG may be in short supply, we can expect the global LNG spread to widen. In turn, this should increase the price of gas at Henry hub, widening the differential to Waha. With roughly 0.5 BCF/d of unhedged volume exposure, this dynamic could again be very significant for APA's near-term free cash flows.

Valuation

I'll start this discussion by looking at EV:EBITDA, and then shift my focus to Price to FCF. As shown in the chart below, APA continues to trade at a significant discount to its peers who are focused solely on North American operations.

By trading at just three times EBITDA, APA falls squarely in value territory. In my opinion, the discount here is far too wide just for the sole fact that APA has assets in Egypt and the North Sea. I see this as a very loose reason to pay 60% more for every dollar of EBITDA generation from APA's peers.

While EBITDA is an important metric, the conversion of EBITDA to FCF is potentially even more important. In this area we can see a similar discount to Devon Energy (DVN) and Ovintiv (OVV). Investors are paying a 50% premium on every dollar of free cash flow generated out of these firms over APA.

Both of these distinct data sets show that APA trades at a significant discount to its peers. Should it trade at a discount because of its foreign exposure? Perhaps. But by trading at pennies on the dollar to its peers, I have a strong conviction that APA is undervalued.

This argument becomes more convincing when factoring in the long-term potential of the Suriname project. Typically, the market pays up for the potential of such a large growth project ($500m in FCF growth by 2029). However, we are not seeing any premium for APA's shares, implying deep value compared to the broader market.

Risks

Geopolitical factors are the most influential topic in the current oil market. Massive market disruptions are underway that have the potential to shift the market from oversupplied to undersupplied almost overnight.

Oil has surged from the low-$60 range, to the upper $70s in just a few weeks (CL1:COM). Now that the Strait of Hormuz has been closed by the Iranian government, time is the most influential factor. The longer the world is forced to operate without a major oil artery, the larger the depletion on global inventories.

This could be a significant event for the industry. However, its impacts could be just as quick to dissipate as they arrived. A short resolution will unwind any geopolitical premium and allow trade flows to restore to normal. Such an event would likely allow crude and LNG prices to return to the early February levels causing a correction in the energy sector.

Investor Key Takeaways

1. APA increased free cash flow generation by 19% in 2025, despite lowering crude oil prices.

2. This performance was created by a strong effort on cost controls. As a side effect to these efforts, the company was able to pull in economic inventory, fully replacing 2025's production.

3. Deeper Permian benches and other non-core targets represent what APA deems "technical upside", giving APA a long runway of inventory.

4. APA's unique gas portfolio affords it multiple avenues to participate in the growing need for natural gas. Margin can be extracted at the well head, at the gulf coast, or in Egypt, giving the company multiple options to grow FCF even in a flat oil market.

5. APA continues to be undervalued compared to peers and future growth prospects do not appear to be appropriately priced into the stock price.

6. I continue to rate APA as a BUY. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz may significantly ramp APA's profitability.

data.title: Hewlett Packard Enterprise: Strong AI-Led Growth Setup
data.description:
Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (HPE) is gearing up to report fiscal first quarter 2026 results on Monday, March 9, 2026, and the setup could not be better, in my opinion. The enterprise-focused server maker will likely publish a massive addition to its AI system backlog, and the expansion of the HPE GreenLake hybrid Cloud platform could provide vital impulses for investors to push shares of HPE into a new up-leg.

Given that CapEx spending has been exploding lately, and projections continue to be highly bullish in the Data Center market, the server company is on track to potentially report record results and materially add to its AI-driven revenue backlog. Shares of Hewlett Packard Enterprise are cheap, trading at just 7.9X forward earnings, which in my opinion translates into a risk profile that is highly skewed to the upside.

Previous rating

Hewlett Packard Enterprise is gearing up to report fiscal first quarter 2026 results on Monday, March 9, 2026, and the setup could not be better, in my opinion. The enterprise-focused server maker will likely publish a massive addition to its AI system backlog, and the expansion of the HPE GreenLake hybrid Cloud platform could provide vital impulses for investors to push shares of HPE into a new up-leg.

Given that CapEx spending has been exploding lately, and projections continue to be highly bullish in the Data Center market, the server company is on track to potentially report record results and materially add to its AI-driven revenue backlog. Shares of Hewlett Packard Enterprise are cheap, trading at just 7.9X forward earnings, which in my opinion translates into a risk profile that is highly skewed to the upside.

All eyes on HPE's AI-driven growth

Hewlett Packard Enterprise is expected to report $0.59 per-share in non-GAAP earnings for Q1 '26 next Monday which implies a year-over-year growth rate of 20%. The top line figure, as per Seeking Alpha's consensus estimates, sits at $9.35B, at the top end of HPE's quarterly revenue guidance ($9.0-9.4B). The estimate trend is also widely positive with analysts upgrading their predictions 11 times in the last 90 days (vs. just 1 EPS estimate down-grade).

I expect the hardware platform to deliver strong results for the first fiscal quarter, especially in its networking division, which saw 150% year-over-year growth in the previous quarter, mainly because of the $14.0B acquisition of Juniper Networks in July 2025. This acquisition pivoted HPE’s business direction from a server-heavy hardware provider to a dominant force in AI-native networking.

The networking market is seeing strong growth, driven by Data Center Networks and Security, which is set to support Hewlett Packard Enterprise's results next week. In addition to core networking segment performance, investors will also be looking forward whether or not HPE will be able to capture more synergies from the Juniper Networks acquisition as the enterprise previously guided for $600M in annualized synergies all the way through FY 2028.

Most importantly, the company has a good chance to make an impression with regards to its AI orders... which I previously indicated were surging: in Q4 '25, the company's total AI orders stood at $13.4B and they have marked consistently new successive highs in the last three years. For Q1 '26, HPE is likely going to continue to report a surging AI orders and a record AI order backlog.

While I expect the company to report revenue at the top end of guidance (~$9.4B), HPE will likely been able to post a sequential improvement in its gross margin amid an ongoing shift to higher margin Cloud services. While Dell (DELL) has the largest business in terms of total gross profits, HPE is actually the most profitable one, with a gross profit margin in excess of 30%.

The main reason for this is the acquisition of Juniper Networks and the HPE GreenLake edge-to-cloud platform which is attracting more enterprise users. In the last quarter, HPE had about 46k users of GreenLake and the company will likely have added at least a couple of hundred lucrative enterprise accounts to its platform in the last quarter, potentially allowing the company to report a sequential increase in its gross margin on Monday.

HPE's valuation implies high safety margin

Shares of Hewlett Packard Enterprise are currently priced at 7.9X forward earnings and have seen sideways stock movements so far this year. Dell already reported blowout earnings last week, so I believe HPE, which benefits from the same underlying growth drivers in the AI-optimized server market, is set for a strong earnings report card.

Dell’s results were driven by strong growth in its key Infrastructure Solutions Group and the enterprise saw a massive bump in its AI-driven server orders. Dell currently has a forward P/E ratio of 10.2X which makes it one of the most affordable hardware companies benefiting from AI-led spending in the Data Center market. For comparison, Super Micro Computer (SMCI) is trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 10.4X which translates to an industry group average P/E ratio for these three hardware giants of approximately 9.3X.

I believe that Hewlett-Packard Enterprise, just like Dell, is worthy of a higher earnings multiplier, especially as AI orders are taking off and there is no slowdown in hyperscaler-driven CapEx spending. This backdrop provides long term fundamental support for growth in the fastest-growing business for HPE.

Hewlett Packard Enterprise could be trading at a 15.0X P/E ratio long term, in my opinion, with the Q1 '26 earnings report next Monday potentially being a big catalyst. Dell's shares, for instance, soared 22% after the server maker's reported last quarter earnings a week ago, mainly due to accelerating AI-optimized server revenue growth. A ~15.0X P/E ratio for HPE calculates to a fair market value of Hewlett-Packard Enterprise's shares of $41/share (implying 90% upside potential).

Primary risks with HPE

The biggest risk for Hewlett-Packard Enterprise, in my opinion, would be slowing adoption momentum of the GreenLake Cloud platform which is where the company is seeing strong growth momentum and which drives the company’s results in the important Hybrid Cloud segment. What would get me to downgrade shares of Hewlett Packard Enterprise is if the company saw a slowdown in AI order growth or if it failed to expand its gross margins next week.

Final thoughts

Given that Dell reported blowout earnings for its server business last week -- which is part of its out-performing Infrastructure Solutions Group -- resulting in record revenue, it is highly likely that Hewlett Packard Enterprise will also submit an impressive earnings scorecard for Q1 ’26 next week.

I am looking forward to seeing sequential gross margin growth for Q1 '26, amid a shift towards higher margin Cloud services, strong customer acquisition for GreenLake and a confirmation of the firm's synergy potential resulting from the Juniper Networks acquisition.

Further, HPE shares are extremely cheap, and have upside potential if the company reports solid AI-driven revenue growth on Monday. I see a favorable investment setup ahead of the Q1 '26 earnings report and continue to recommend HPE as a Buy for investors with a long term investment horizon.

data.title: Caterpillar: Everyone Is Betting On AI Bottlenecks, There Is One Being Overlooked
data.description:
Investment Thesis

There are two major tailwinds to Caterpillar (NYSE: CAT), a legacy American construction and energy engineering equipment manufacturer.

  • The consistent and macro one is that the Company is poised to benefit from President Trump's domestic industrial policy, increase in homebuilding activities, as well as the United States' global oil production push - but these macros are already priced in (to be discussed in the valuation part) since Trump's term, so this is not the thesis we will elaborate on today.
  • The opportunistic tailwind is the "AI bottleneck play" that rotates upward of the supply chain—from - from GPUs components to AI data centers and, ultimately, electricity

Major AI bottleneck is energy

When AI infrastructure is being developed rapidly, we start to see "bottlenecks" that are limiting the Moore's Law being played out. Electricity is one of them. To simplify the problem of electricity in one sentence is:

Traditional model of building the data center first, connecting to the grid after, is no longer feasible

This is because the current grid infrastructure, which has been built for over a decade, can no longer handle the exponentially growing electricity demand, if not being destroyed. There are two major reasons:

Transmission is a larger problem than generation

The electricity demand of AI is constantly high and growing, and data centers run 24/7. However, the conventional electricity generation capacity is either being planned slowly or is "intermittent" which is the major characteristic of solar and wind. But in either case, they are "far from where the demands are", which makes transmission difficult and overloaded. If you see the below roadmap of conventional electricity generation, you will understand how many "lines" we need for the many gigawatt-hours of electricity we need.

You can think of the transmission problem as a highway built decades ago catering to the numbers of cars in modern society—traffic jams and depreciation happen very quickly. In fact, there are 2,300 gigawatts of total generation waiting from ~10,300 projects to be connected to the grid as of 2024. What's worse is that, in some states where AI data centers are being built, it requires 7 years of average waiting time before it can start.

To an AI company, this is like telling them to wait for 7 years before it can further scale up and realize the revenue. The cruel fact is Moore's Law of AI development can't wait that long.

Reliability requires a full-grid upgrade

To AI data centers, especially those critical hyperscalers, they require as much quantity as quality - hence reliability is an issue. Another stat in the US shows that the average system interruption duration in 2024 is around 10 hours per year, according to the EIA.

To a reliability-first data center, it cannot afford a 10-second outage, not to mention an average outage rate at the unit of hours. Therefore, hyperscalers are looking for more cost-efficient and reliable electricity generation methods that can substitute the old infrastructure, which is grid-based.

This is where investment opportunities lie.

On-site electricity: The Holy Grail of Computing Energy Source

When the grid is no longer sufficient, when the traditional central generation model is slow, and when long-range transmission is cost-inefficient, "on-site generation" solves the problem, and this is why companies like Bloom Energy forecast more than 25% of electricity supply to data centers to be "on-site".

Despite sacrificing the economy of scale of electricity generation (through large-scale, remote generation plants), it offers two incomparable advantages, which are "timeliness" (skipping the year-long planning and connection time) and "certainty" (unreliable grid). Therefore, this is why companies are chasing after the trend.

The Strength of Caterpillar

There is an investable angle for Caterpillar other than its typical "yellow machinery" impressions in homebuilding and mining—reciprocating engines / Generator.

In 2024, the company showed a pivotal business milestone: for the first time of in its history, revenue of the energy and transportation segment exceeded that of the construction machinery segment, at $28.9 billion. This is the reason why CEO Joseph Creed mentioned in its latest earnings that energy is the largest and fastest-growing segment, driven by strong demand for turbines in data centers. Caterpillar's technology backbone is its "turbine" used in the top industrial machinery. Extending its strength into a new, lucrative business application, which is AI, is the most reasonable business strategy.

Caterpillar, unlike scaled generation plants or other modular energy sources, has an agile approach in deploying machinery in remote areas where new AI data centers are being built. Delivery of these turbine generators is also faster than its competitors in "on-site generation" like Bloom Energy, which needs a long turnaround for its energy storage boxes.

Valuation: The market has not priced in such opportunities

One straightforward way of valuing an amateur-stage company is to calculate from its margin, growth, and reinvestment rate to determine the value of the business from its engines of profitability, efficiency, and risk. As of year-end 2025, its financial metrics are

TTM Revenue $67.59 billion
Net Profit Margin (TTM) 13.1%
ROE 45.1%
Cost of Equity 7 - 7.5%

Using the above parameters and reverse-engineer the implied growth rate from its current share price at around $700, the market is pricing in a growth rate at merely ~4.5%. With the AI sector and its associated energy demands at double-digit annual growth rate in the next decades, this shows a significant undervaluation on Caterpillar as it's still being assumed at a 4.5% growth.

PEG ratio also shows that Caterpillar is significantly more fairly valued than GEV, which is another AI energy bottleneck trade.

Investment Risks

  • AI bubble: Largest risk of the company is the burst of the AI bubble, when valuation of all AI-related companies has skyrocketed in a bull market. However, given the volatility causes by the broader market sentiment worrying about the realistic value of AI companies, Caterpillar will be affected once valuation is to be rationalize
  • Economy downturn: With regional wars, conflicts and sticky inflation looming, US economy still faces challenges which might see a downturn. As a heavy industrial cyclical stock, Caterpillar will be affected by the macro market economic environment
  • New technologies: The thesis for Caterpillar is based on the fact that is offers agile and economical solutions. However, the likes of small modular nuclear reactor and battery technologies may improve efficiency significantly, making Caterpillar's more traditional solution obsolete

Neal Rosenberg

Ron Baron

Portfolio Manager

CEO, Portfolio Manager

Dear Baron Growth Fund Shareholder,

Performance

Baron Growth Fund® (the Fund)(BGRFX) declined 2.69% (Institutional Shares) for the quarter ended December 31, 2025. This trailed the return of the Fund’s benchmark, the Russell 2000 Growth Index (the Benchmark), which gained 1.22% for the quarter. The Russell 3000 Index, which measures the performance of the 3,000 largest publicly traded U.S. companies, gained 2.40% for the quarter.

Our strategy of owning a portfolio of competitively advantaged small and medium-sized businesses remained out of favor for most of this quarter. We observed an improvement in early December, as investors showed some renewed enthusiasm for the high-quality stocks (as defined by MSCI Barra) that exclusively populate our Fund. We believe that the valuation of the portfolio is particularly compelling given the significant divergence between its consistent earnings growth and its recent performance.

The Fund endured an unusually challenging year. Since March 31, the Fund declined 10.37%, which compares to a 27.15% gain in the Benchmark. We attribute approximately 60% of our relative underperformance to several positions where the market’s assessment of AI risk differed from our own. We attribute most of the remaining relative underperformance to our focus on owning leading businesses in their sectors, which has been the foundation of our strategy since inception.

We believe that our practice of exclusively investing in businesses with superior characteristics has been the primary driver of the Fund’s favorable long-term results, which has exceeded the performance of the Benchmark by 354 basis points annually since inception. In 2025, this strategy was out of favor, as investors elected to sell higher-quality investments like those that we own to buy riskier stocks. The magnitude of this shift was historically notable. According to data from MSCI Barra, performance for the Earnings Quality factor during the nine-month period ended December 8, 2025 was in the 100th percentile since 1975, which is when data first became available. Conversely, the positive performance of the Beta and Residual Volatility for this period are in the 1st and 6th percentiles, respectively. The few instances where Beta and Residual Volatility have performed better occurred mostly during the dotcom and post-COVID rallies.

We also experienced declines in several stocks where the market’s assessment of AI’s impact differed from our own. This cohort, which includes stocks such as Gartner, Inc. (IT), CoStar Group, Inc. (CSGP), Clearwater Analytics Holdings, Inc. (CWAN), FactSet Research Systems Inc. (FDS), MSCI Inc. (MSCI), and Guidewire Software, Inc. (GWRE), presently represents approximately 42% of our portfolio. On average, these stocks declined 15% in 2025 despite producing 10% revenue growth and 15% EPS growth. This significant divergence between growth and stock performance represents valuation compression, primarily due to investor concerns that AI will disrupt these companies in the future. We remain optimistic that all boast vast total addressable markets (TAM), sustainable competitive advantages, and attractive and durable growth prospects. We expect their valuations to expand as these characteristics are again recognized by the market.

We were encouraged by the improvement in our relative performance in December, as our style started to come back into favor and the market began to re-evaluate some of the names that it had previously deemed AI losers. We observed a change in the market in early December following mixed earnings results from AI-high fliers Oracle and Broadcom. From December 8 through December 31, the Fund outperformed by 635 basis points, with the Fund’s style biases, specifically Earnings Quality, Beta, and Residual Volatility being positive drivers of results. The average return for the cohort of stocks in the portfolio that had been deemed AI-losers outperformed the market by approximately 7% over that period. While that condition has not sustained early in 2026, we believe it demonstrates the significant potential for outperformance when our style is in favor.

We believe that the recent performance of Clearwater exemplifies the impact of the “AI narrative” on stock prices and demonstrates the dramatic upside potential of these investments as the market rethinks its assumptions. Clearwater is a leading provider of investment accounting and analytics services delivered via cloud-native software. Clearwater serves a vast TAM that is at least 10 times larger than its current scale. Management has built a remarkable financial model, underpinned by 20% organic revenue growth, margins in the mid-30% range, robust free cash flow generation, and near-perfect retention rates. In late 2024, shares of Clearwater peaked around $33 per share, up almost 80% from its IPO price just three years before. In early 2025, the company announced acquisitions that further expanded its TAM, bolstered its product portfolio, and positioned it to sustain or accelerate its earnings growth over time. Despite bright long-term prospects, investors began to view Clearwater as a generic AI-loser. Shares declined consistently over the course of 2025, reaching a low below $16 in early November. This represented a 50% peak-to-trough decline in share price despite the company growing its EBITDA almost 33% organically and almost 70% when including acquisitions.

Despite the stock’s decline, we assessed the likelihood of portfolio accountants choosing to write their own software as minimal. Clearwater’s financial results continued to prove this out, and the stock began to rally off its lows, although the price remained significantly disconnected from our assessment of fair value. In December, a consortium of private equity firms announced a deal to acquire Clearwater for $8.4 billion, which we view as a high-conviction refutation of the AI-loser narrative. The takeout is coming at a 47% premium to Clearwater’s pre-deal price and an approximately 55% premium to its November low, although we still believe that this price undervalues the business. We are optimistic that all our AI-impacted investments will develop similarly to Clearwater.

We believe that the portfolio is well positioned to generate compelling returns on a go-forward basis. Over time, we believe that the performance of stocks closely tracks the growth of the underlying businesses. In 2025, we estimate that the portfolio grew its earnings by 12% on average. However, the Fund declined 14% for the year, which implies that the portfolio’s aggregate valuation contracted by 26%. This is despite the Fund generating higher margins, cash flow, and returns on capital versus its historical composition and our Benchmark. We are optimistic that as the market’s appreciation for high quality businesses returns and its perspective on AI’s impact more closely aligns with our own, the Fund’s valuation will expand, enhancing the returns we already expect from consistent compounded earnings growth. We believe that the 635 basis points of outperformance generated in just three weeks demonstrates the significant pent-up potential currently embedded in the portfolio.

The table below groups our portfolio based on our assessment of the attributes that best characterize each investment. While this does not perfectly correlate to the Global Industry Classification Standard, the industry standard nomenclature, we believe it provides added transparency into our thought process.
